item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen hooks aplenty in this site. But few of them really made me go "oh shiiit" and were so completely unexpected to me as this one. You really went the extra mile for this story and it shows. Congratulations! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they

/ðeɪ/

pronoun

1. used to refer to two or more people or things previously mentioned or easily identified.
"the two men could get life sentences if they are convicted"
2. used to refer to a person of unspecified gender.
"ask a friend if they could help"

"They" is not necessarily a singular pronoun. However, since this issue's been brought up twice, I'll change it to "he".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The use of plural pronouns to refer to a singular entity is driving me nuts.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so on reading this article I was immediately reminded of SCP-1628 as both involve massive automaton replications of historical periods and frankly its a pretty easy crosslink to suggest design similarities between the batches of drones. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Partial diagram of an SCP-2668-1 instance, recovered from beneath the Capitoline Museums, Rome. Believed to date back to the late 16th century.

To this:

Partial diagram of an SCP-2668-1 instance, recovered from beneath the Capitoline Museums, Rome. Believed to date back to the late 16th century. Design similarities to instances of SCP-1628 have been noted.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because, while he's still classified as SCP-2668-3, the standard 'it' would lessen the compassionate tone I was aiming for. They don't refer to him as -3 multiple times because it would be jarring flow-wise, and more difficult to read.

In-universe, just imagine they partially waive the 'disconnected, unemotionless tone' requirement for people the IU author would have built up a friendship with.

EDIT: I also fully support the use of third person singular genderless 'they' in technical documents, if that was what you were asking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is Forth referred to as "they" in the addendum when clearly identified as a man? If it's because of his designation is -3, then should they be referring to him by name? If they are ignoring that in honor of his passing, why not wave it entirely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wise words, though not so relevant in the context of "eternal gladiatorial combat with an unkillable monster made of forever-shitting ladybugs" :P

item.title: Staff post - CLOSED. Do not reply to this post.
item.content:encoded: Abbetik, don't use slurs such as "retarded" - it's against the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Ever tried. Ever failed. No matter. Try again. Fail again. Fail better."
Samuel Beckett

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I mean if he couldn't beat them in his prime, what chance does he stand at 70+? Your choice of SCP-2794… I didn't really read the skip so I didn't understand what you were going for until I came here. SCP-682 is retarded, and I am glad that you did not use it. I'm not sure how you would do it better, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done, enjoyed it from top to bottom. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not quite sure I get all the details about where this ends up, but it was an interesting road to get there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It's written very well, the ending's poignant in a way that doesn't drag, and while the core concept is great, that ending touch of being locked in mortal combat with an interdimensional shit-bug monster for all is, depending on your worldview, either supremely gratifying or incomprehensibly terrifying.

As a fan of gladiator stuff I approve of this. It's fun and sad and scary. Can't ask for much else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Urgh. I've got a 'sink or swim' mentality regarding this one. Let's see how it goes!

Thanks to Kakroom, Buckyboy, taylor_itkin, DarkStuff, Mendelssohn, LadyKatie, toastedraptor, and SunnyClockwork for all giving various degrees of crit.

+ Regarding SCP-2794
- Hide this

SCP-2794 was used in this article with Fingo7's permission. I wanted a scip that couldn't be killed, and really, really didn't want to use that one lizard boi. I don't think it leans on SCP-2794 too heavily, given that it could literally be any other immortal scip, but I'm open to feedback on that front.

+ Regarding spoilers
- Hide this

So. It's animatronic robot Romans that drag people into a coliseum where they can fight their imagined enemies for as long as they need to to win. Fair enough. Forth, on the other hand, is stuck fighting something immortal, due to his exposure to anomalies and 2794 in particular.

After a long, long time, he breaks out, using s c i e n c e, and goes back to the Foundation, living a long and happy life. Then he dies, his body demanifests, and activity is observed from within the arena again. Do I need to spell it out? :P

I also think it says something about me as a person that my first attempt at writing an author avatar-esque character ended badly, with said character's permanent entrapment in a battle with pooping bugs. Ah well.

Image is from here, licensed under CC BY 4.0.
